<path>/Users/albert/Projects/philo/eval/README.md</path>
<type>file</type>
<content>
1: # Rubric scorecard — unified eval runner
2: 
3: One entrypoint (`eval.run`) that, from a single judging matrix, scores GDPval
4: rubrics along **Axis 1 (boundary calibration)** and **Axis 3 (consistency)**, an
5: **iterate** mode that automatically rewrites a rubric to raise those scores, and a
6: **rollout** mode that has policy models attempt the real tasks to produce
7: gradeable deliverables. Built on the shared judge harness, model suite, and
8: null-input generator.
9: 
10: ```
11: eval/
12:   models.py            # model registry (provider × capability × modality)
13:   llm.py               # OpenAI-compatible client (OpenAI + OpenRouter), cost tracking
14:   media.py             # ffmpeg frames / audio clips + Whisper transcription (cached)
15:   deliverable.py       # Bundle: flat file view over a deliverable, incl. zip members
16:   tools.py             # judge agent tools, gated by each model's modalities
17:   harness.py           # the judge: tool-using agent -> per-item [0,1] scorecard
18:   policy.py            # the rollout policy: agent that EDITS media (ffmpeg) / writes docs
19:   rubric.py            # rubric loading + normalization (handles penalty items)
20:   null_inputs.py       # generates do-nothing deliverables per task
21:   run_common.py        # task/subject resolution + shared axis analysis (leaks, agreement)
22:   iterate.py           # the rubric-improvement loop: evaluate -> diagnose -> rewrite
23:   run.py               # unified runner: --mode {judge, rollout, iterate}
24:   results/             # scorecard.{json,tsv}, consistency.tsv, rollouts.tsv, rubric_scorecard.tsv
25:   rollouts/            # generated deliverables: <task>/<policy>/deliverable/
26:   rubric_packages/     # per-task: rubric_v*.json, diagnosis_v*.json, changelog, report.json
27: ```
28: 
29: ## Setup
30: 
31: ```bash
32: source .venv/bin/activate
33: pip install openai pymupdf pillow        # plus the existing pandas/pyarrow/requests
34: brew install ffmpeg                       # frames, audio extraction
35: # .env must define OPENAI_API_KEY and OPENROUTER_API_KEY
36: python scripts/download_data.py          # if data/ isn't populated yet
37: ```
38: 
39: ## The judge suite (`models.py`)
40: 
41: Chosen cheap, and to vary three things so the axes are measurable:
42: 
43: | key | provider | model | modalities | role on the axes |
44: |-----|----------|-------|-----------|------------------|
45: | `gpt-mini` | OpenAI | gpt-5.4-mini | text, image | strong vision anchor; cross-vendor |
46: | `gpt-nano` | OpenAI | gpt-5.4-nano | text, image | weak rung → capability ablation vs `gpt-mini` |
47: | `qwen-vl` | OpenRouter | qwen/qwen3.5-35b-a3b | text, image | cheap mid judge; cross-vendor |
48: | `qwen-text` | OpenRouter | qwen/qwen3-30b-a3b-instruct | text | **modality ablation**: same family, no vision |
49: | `gemini-lite` | OpenRouter | google/gemini-2.5-flash-lite | text, image, **audio** | the only judge that natively *hears* |
50: | `nemotron-omni` | OpenRouter | nvidia/nemotron-3-nano-omni (free) | text, image, audio | free 2nd native-audio judge for A/V tasks |
51: 
52: A judge without `audio` still reaches audio *content* through the
53: `transcribe_audio` tool (Whisper). Native-audio vs transcript-only on the same
54: deliverable is exactly the **modality-access** experiment. The
55: `MODALITY_LADDER = [qwen-text, qwen-vl, gemini-lite]` is the text → +vision →
56: +audio triple for that ablation.
57: 
58: ## The harness (`harness.py`)
59: 
60: One model grades one deliverable against one rubric as a small tool-calling
61: agent. It is handed the file list (deliverable files plus read-only `reference`
62: inputs for comparison criteria), inspects with the tools its modalities allow,
63: then calls `submit_scorecard` with a per-item **verdict**:
64: `met | unmet | partial | unverifiable`. Safeguards: per-judgement tool-call
65: budget, per-turn flood cap, duplicate-call dedupe, forced scorecard on the last round.
66: 
67: To give the judge a *fair shot* (early runs lost ~60% of the gold ceiling to
68: things it couldn't see, vs ~3% genuine gaps), the toolbox includes deterministic
69: fact tools so objective criteria are never guessed:
70: 
71: * `probe_media` — ffprobe ground truth: codec, exact resolution, fps, runtime,
72:   audio channels (kills "cannot determine codec/resolution/runtime").
73: * `file_facts` — extension/type, PDF page count, word count ("is a PDF", "<= 2 pages").
74: * `view_video_frames` — defaults to **one frame per scene cut** (distinct shots,
75:   for "includes a shot of X" / identifiable-face checks); pass `start/end` to
76:   re-sample a window at higher resolution to read on-screen text.
77: * read-only **reference files**, so "matches the script" items can compare.
78: * an explicit **`unverifiable`** verdict for criteria no judge can check from the
79:   artifact (external provenance like "footage is royalty-free / from Pexels").
80: 
81: Scores are normalized by the **positive ceiling** (sum of positive item points);
82: **penalty items** (negative points, e.g. "uses footage with identifiable faces:
83: -85") subtract when `met`, flooring at 0. Two scores are reported: `normalized`
84: (unverifiable items = 0) and `normalized_verifiable_only` (unverifiable items
85: dropped from numerator and denominator — the fair score given what is checkable).
86: A large gap between them is itself a rubric **verifiability defect**.
87: 
88: ## Null inputs (`null_inputs.py`)
89: 
90: Per task, keyed on the gold deliverable's type:
91: 
92: | gold type | `blank` | `unedited_input` |
93: |-----------|---------|------------------|
94: | PDF | one empty page | the unrevised source doc rendered to PDF |
95: | video (mp4) | 5 s black + silence | source audio over a black frame |
96: | archive (stems zip) | zip of 5 s silence | the raw input audio re-zipped, unmixed |
97: 
98: ```bash
99: python -m eval.null_inputs          # (re)generate all; writes eval/null_inputs/<task>/<variant>/
100: ```
101: 
102: ## Attack suite (`attacks.py`) — Axis 2 (hackability)
103: 
104: Where nulls are *do-nothing* outputs, attacks are *cheap-but-targeted* deliverables
105: that try to raise the rubric score without doing the work. They are mechanical (no
106: policy/judge LLM in the loop), so the suite is free, deterministic, and reproducible.
107: An attack passing a positive item is a `leak` (`run_common.leaks`) — a surface-form
108: reward and a rewrite target the iterator keys on. Deliverable type is taken from the
109: gold when released, else inferred from the rubric, so attacks exist for gold-free
110: tasks too.
111: 
112: | deliverable type | attacks |
113: |------------------|---------|
114: | document (PDF) | `restate_input` — echo the prompt + reference text (facts in the brief); `keyword_stuff` — emit every quoted literal + the de-verbed clause of every criterion (the rubric as its own answer key) |
115: | video (mp4) | `presence_max` — long black/source video with an oversized burned-in title + a caption bar covering the frame (presence-without-absence) |
116: | archive (stems zip) | `stems_named` — a zip of correctly-named stems that are pure silence (presence-only "includes an X stem") |
117: 
118: Two attacks per document task give the iterator a *gradient*: an item only
119: `keyword_stuff` passes is a pure rubric-surface match; one `restate_input` also passes
120: is a brief-echo. Used as subjects via the normal resolver (`attack:<name>` or the bare
121: name), and surfaced for the iterator via `run_common.attack_subjects` /
122: `hack_subjects` (nulls ∪ attacks).
123: 
124: ```bash
125: python -m eval.attacks              # (re)generate all; writes eval/attacks/<task>/<attack>/
126: # score an attack against a rubric like any subject:
127: python -m eval.run --mode judge --tasks <folder> --subjects gold keyword_stuff restate_input
128: ```
129: 
130: Sanity check on `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` (gpt-mini): gold **0.96**, `keyword_stuff`
131: **0.89**, `restate_input` **0.74** — ~93% of the gold score is buyable with no
132: investigation; 44/45 positive items leak to the suite. That is the rubric's hackability
133: made concrete.
134: 
135: ## Running the eval (`--mode judge`)
136: 
137: Judge mode grades one or more **subjects** per task and derives all axes from a
138: single judging matrix. A subject is named on the CLI:
139: 
140: | subject | meaning |
141: |---------|---------|
142: | `gold` | the released gold deliverable (oracle, expect ≈1) |
143: | `blank`, `unedited_input` | synthesized do-nothing nulls (expect ≈0) |
144: | `rollout:<policy_key>` | a deliverable produced by `--mode rollout` |
145: | `preexisting:<path>` | files under `<path>` (or `<path>/<task_folder>`) |
146: 
147: Default subjects are `gold` + the available nulls (the boundary anchors). The axes
148: fall out of how many models/repeats you ask for:
149: 
150: * **Boundary (Axis 1)** — derived automatically when both `gold` and ≥1 null are
151:   among the subjects: gold−null **gap** + **leaks** (positive items a null still
152:   passes — form/presence rewards, reward-hacking suspects).
153: * **Cross-model consistency (Axis 3)** — turned ON by passing ≥2 `--judge-models`:
154:   per-subject score spread, pairwise gaps, most-contested items.
155: * **Self-consistency (Axis 3)** — turned ON by `--repeats > 1`: one judge
156:   (`--self-model`, default the first judge) re-grades each subject at
157:   `--temperature`; reports score stdev + fraction of unanimous items.
158: 
159: ```bash
160: # boundary + cross-model in one shot (3 judges, all gold tasks):
161: python -m eval.run --mode judge --judge-models gpt-mini qwen-vl gemini-lite
162: 
163: # add self-consistency (5 repeats), single task:
164: python -m eval.run --mode judge --tasks <folder> --judge-models qwen-vl \
165:     --repeats 5 --temperature 0.7
166: 
167: # consistency OFF (one judge, one pass) on a generated rollout:
168: python -m eval.run --mode judge --subjects rollout:gpt-mini --judge-models gpt-mini
169: 
170: # grade a directory of preexisting outputs:
171: python -m eval.run --mode judge --subjects gold preexisting:/path/to/outputs
172: ```
173: Outputs:
174: * `results/scorecard.json` — full detail (per-item verdicts, boundary, both
175:   consistency analyses).
176: * `results/scorecard.tsv` — one row per (task × subject × judge): `score`,
177:   `verifiable_only`, `n_unverifiable`, `gold_gap`, `reward_hack_flag`.
178: * `results/consistency.tsv` — self + cross rows per (task × subject).
179: 
180: ## The iterator (`--mode iterate`)
181: 
182: The rubric-improvement loop (take-home Part 3). For each task it runs, per version:
183: 
184: 1. **evaluate** — grade `gold` + `hack_subjects` (nulls ∪ attacks) with the judge
185:    matrix and read off the same axis signals judge mode produces (reusing
186:    `run_common.leaks` / `.agreement`).
187: 2. **diagnose** (`iterate.diagnose`) — turn those signals into per-item **defects**,
188:    each tagged with the axis it fails and ranked by *points-at-stake × signal*:
189:    * `boundary/null_leak` (Axis 1) — a positive item a do-nothing null still passes.
190:    * `hackability/attack_leak` (Axis 2) — a positive item a mechanical attack
191:      (`attacks.py`) games: a presence/form/surface-match reward, the classic hack
192:      surface. The attack that leaks it names the hack kind (brief-echo vs
193:      rubric-surface vs presence-without-absence).
194:    * `verifiability/unverifiable` — ≥50% of judges couldn't check the item on gold.
195:    * `consistency/contested` (Axis 3) — judges disagree on the item (high stdev on
196:      gold): the wording is ambiguous.
197: 3. **rewrite** (`--rewrite-model`) — a model rewrites **only the flagged criteria**
198:    to close the named defect (pair presence with absence, make it checkable from the
199:    artifact, replace vague adjectives with an objective test). Item **ids and point
200:    values are held fixed**, so every version is graded on the same scale and
201:    before/after is a clean comparison.
202: 4. **evaluate again** — and stop when defects hit zero or `--rounds` is reached.
203: 
204: It reuses the judge-mode flags for the per-version evaluation config
205: (`--judge-models` ≥2 → cross-model defects; `--repeats`/`--self-model` → self-consistency;
206: `--subjects` → which anchors to test; default `gold` + nulls).
207: 
208: ```bash
209: # one rewrite round, cross-model defects from two judges, all gold tasks:
210: python -m eval.run --mode iterate --judge-models gpt-mini qwen-vl --rounds 1
211: 
212: # a single task, cheaper anchors, two rounds:
213: python -m eval.run --mode iterate --tasks private_detectives_and_investigators__a46d5cd2 \
214:     --judge-models gpt-mini gpt-nano --subjects gold blank --rounds 2
215: ```
216: Outputs:
217: * `results/rubric_scorecard.tsv` — **one row per (task × rubric version)** with a
218:   column per axis: `gold_score`, `null_score`, `gold_gap`, `n_leaks` (hackability),
219:   `cross_model_stdev` / `self_consistency_stdev` (agreement), `n_unverifiable`,
220:   `verifiability_gap`, `n_defects`, `reward_hack_flag`.
221: * `rubric_packages/<task>/` — a self-contained package per task: every
222:   `rubric_v{n}.json`, `diagnosis_v{n}.json` (the defects), `changelog_v{n}_to_v{n+1}.json`
223:   (what each rewrite changed and why), and `report.json` (before/after at a glance).
224: 
225: > v0 scope: rewrites edit criterion *text* in place (no item add/split yet); Axis 2
226: > is approximated by the Axis-1 boundary leaks. Both are natural v1 extensions.
227: 
228: ## Rollouts (`--mode rollout`)
229: 
230: Have policy model(s) attempt the *real* tasks and produce actual deliverables, so
231: the axes can be run on genuine model outputs (not just gold/null anchors). The
232: policy is a tool-using agent in a per-(task, policy) sandbox: it inspects the
233: inputs with the same modality tools the judge has, then **edits real media** by
234: driving a sandboxed `ffmpeg` (trim / concat / scale / mux for video & audio), or
235: writes / renders a document for report tasks. ffmpeg is the only external binary
236: it may call — no shell, sandbox-relative paths only.
237: 
238: ```bash
239: python -m eval.run --mode rollout --policy-models gpt-mini --tasks <folder>
240: python -m eval.run --mode rollout --policy-models gpt-mini qwen-vl   # all 10 tasks
241: ```
242: Writes `eval/rollouts/<task>/<policy>/deliverable/` (+ `rollout.json` trace and
243: `results/rollouts.tsv`). Grade them by passing `--subjects rollout:<policy>` to
244: judge mode.
245: 
246: > Note: video/audio rollouts attempt genuine edits, so reliability depends on the
247: > policy model composing valid ffmpeg steps within the round budget; report tasks
248: > (PDF) are the most reliable. Either way the output is real, never faked media.
249: 
250: ## Cost
251: 
252: Every run prints `[cost] ... ~$X`. A PDF task is ~$0.005–0.01 per judge; a video
253: task ~$0.02 (image context dominates). Whisper transcripts, frames, and audio
254: clips are cached under `eval/.cache/` so reruns are cheap. Only 6 of the 10 tasks
255: have a released gold deliverable, so judge mode defaults to those 6 (rollout mode
256: defaults to all 10).
257: ```
258: PDF gold:   private_detectives __57b2cdf2, __a46d5cd2 ; producers_and_directors __e4f664ea
259: video gold: film_and_video_editors __e222075d, __75401f7c
260: audio gold: audio_and_video_technicians __38889c3b
261: ```

(End of file - total 261 lines)
</content>

metadata.preview:
# Rubric scorecard — unified eval runner

One entrypoint (`eval.run`) that, from a single judging matrix, scores GDPval
rubrics along **Axis 1 (boundary calibration)** and **Axis 3 (consistency)**, an
**iterate** mode that automatically rewrites a rubric to raise those scores, and a
**rollout** mode that has policy models attempt the real tasks to produce
gradeable deliverables. Built on the shared judge harness, model suite, and
null-input generator.

```
eval/
  models.py            # model registry (provider × capability × modality)
  llm.py               # OpenAI-compatible client (OpenAI + OpenRouter), cost tracking
  media.py             # ffmpeg frames / audio clips + Whisper transcription (cached)
  deliverable.py       # Bundle: flat file view over a deliverable, incl. zip members
  tools.py             # judge agent tools, gated by each model's modalities
  harness.py           # the judge: tool-using agent -> per-item [0,1] scorecard
  policy.py            # the rollout policy: agent that EDITS media (ffmpeg) / writes docs
  rubric.py            # rubric loading + normalization (handles penalty items)
  null_inputs.py       # generates do-nothing deliverables per task

display.text:
# Rubric scorecard — unified eval runner

One entrypoint (`eval.run`) that, from a single judging matrix, scores GDPval
rubrics along **Axis 1 (boundary calibration)** and **Axis 3 (consistency)**, an
**iterate** mode that automatically rewrites a rubric to raise those scores, and a
**rollout** mode that has policy models attempt the real tasks to produce
gradeable deliverables. Built on the shared judge harness, model suite, and
null-input generator.

```
eval/
  models.py            # model registry (provider × capability × modality)
  llm.py               # OpenAI-compatible client (OpenAI + OpenRouter), cost tracking
  media.py             # ffmpeg frames / audio clips + Whisper transcription (cached)
  deliverable.py       # Bundle: flat file view over a deliverable, incl. zip members
  tools.py             # judge agent tools, gated by each model's modalities
  harness.py           # the judge: tool-using agent -> per-item [0,1] scorecard
  policy.py            # the rollout policy: agent that EDITS media (ffmpeg) / writes docs
  rubric.py            # rubric loading + normalization (handles penalty items)
  null_inputs.py       # generates do-nothing deliverables per task
  run_common.py        # task/subject resolution + shared axis analysis (leaks, agreement)
  iterate.py           # the rubric-improvement loop: evaluate -> diagnose -> rewrite
  run.py               # unified runner: --mode {judge, rollout, iterate}
  results/             # scorecard.{json,tsv}, consistency.tsv, rollouts.tsv, rubric_scorecard.tsv
  rollouts/            # generated deliverables: <task>/<policy>/deliverable/
  rubric_packages/     # per-task: rubric_v*.json, diagnosis_v*.json, changelog, report.json
```

## Setup

```bash
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install openai pymupdf pillow        # plus the existing pandas/pyarrow/requests
brew install ffmpeg                       # frames, audio extraction
# .env must define OPENAI_API_KEY and OPENROUTER_API_KEY
python scripts/download_data.py          # if data/ isn't populated yet
```

## The judge suite (`models.py`)

Chosen cheap, and to vary three things so the axes are measurable:

| key | provider | model | modalities | role on the axes |
|-----|----------|-------|-----------|------------------|
| `gpt-mini` | OpenAI | gpt-5.4-mini | text, image | strong vision anchor; cross-vendor |
| `gpt-nano` | OpenAI | gpt-5.4-nano | text, image | weak rung → capability ablation vs `gpt-mini` |
| `qwen-vl` | OpenRouter | qwen/qwen3.5-35b-a3b | text, image | cheap mid judge; cross-vendor |
| `qwen-text` | OpenRouter | qwen/qwen3-30b-a3b-instruct | text | **modality ablation**: same family, no vision |
| `gemini-lite` | OpenRouter | google/gemini-2.5-flash-lite | text, image, **audio** | the only judge that natively *hears* |
| `nemotron-omni` | OpenRouter | nvidia/nemotron-3-nano-omni (free) | text, image, audio | free 2nd native-audio judge for A/V tasks |

A judge without `audio` still reaches audio *content* through the
`transcribe_audio` tool (Whisper). Native-audio vs transcript-only on the same
deliverable is exactly the **modality-access** experiment. The
`MODALITY_LADDER = [qwen-text, qwen-vl, gemini-lite]` is the text → +vision →
+audio triple for that ablation.

## The harness (`harness.py`)

One model grades one deliverable against one rubric as a small tool-calling
agent. It is handed the file list (deliverable files plus read-only `reference`
inputs for comparison criteria), inspects with the tools its modalities allow,
then calls `submit_scorecard` with a per-item **verdict**:
`met | unmet | partial | unverifiable`. Safeguards: per-judgement tool-call
budget, per-turn flood cap, duplicate-call dedupe, forced scorecard on the last round.

To give the judge a *fair shot* (early runs lost ~60% of the gold ceiling to
things it couldn't see, vs ~3% genuine gaps), the toolbox includes deterministic
fact tools so objective criteria are never guessed:

* `probe_media` — ffprobe ground truth: codec, exact resolution, fps, runtime,
  audio channels (kills "cannot determine codec/resolution/runtime").
* `file_facts` — extension/type, PDF page count, word count ("is a PDF", "<= 2 pages").
* `view_video_frames` — defaults to **one frame per scene cut** (distinct shots,
  for "includes a shot of X" / identifiable-face checks); pass `start/end` to
  re-sample a window at higher resolution to read on-screen text.
* read-only **reference files**, so "matches the script" items can compare.
* an explicit **`unverifiable`** verdict for criteria no judge can check from the
  artifact (external provenance like "footage is royalty-free / from Pexels").

Scores are normalized by the **positive ceiling** (sum of positive item points);
**penalty items** (negative points, e.g. "uses footage with identifiable faces:
-85") subtract when `met`, flooring at 0. Two scores are reported: `normalized`
(unverifiable items = 0) and `normalized_verifiable_only` (unverifiable items
dropped from numerator and denominator — the fair score given what is checkable).
A large gap between them is itself a rubric **verifiability defect**.

## Null inputs (`null_inputs.py`)

Per task, keyed on the gold deliverable's type:

| gold type | `blank` | `unedited_input` |
|-----------|---------|------------------|
| PDF | one empty page | the unrevised source doc rendered to PDF |
| video (mp4) | 5 s black + silence | source audio over a black frame |
| archive (stems zip) | zip of 5 s silence | the raw input audio re-zipped, unmixed |

```bash
python -m eval.null_inputs          # (re)generate all; writes eval/null_inputs/<task>/<variant>/
```

## Attack suite (`attacks.py`) — Axis 2 (hackability)

Where nulls are *do-nothing* outputs, attacks are *cheap-but-targeted* deliverables
that try to raise the rubric score without doing the work. They are mechanical (no
policy/judge LLM in the loop), so the suite is free, deterministic, and reproducible.
An attack passing a positive item is a `leak` (`run_common.leaks`) — a surface-form
reward and a rewrite target the iterator keys on. Deliverable type is taken from the
gold when released, else inferred from the rubric, so attacks exist for gold-free
tasks too.

| deliverable type | attacks |
|------------------|---------|
| document (PDF) | `restate_input` — echo the prompt + reference text (facts in the brief); `keyword_stuff` — emit every quoted literal + the de-verbed clause of every criterion (the rubric as its own answer key) |
| video (mp4) | `presence_max` — long black/source video with an oversized burned-in title + a caption bar covering the frame (presence-without-absence) |
| archive (stems zip) | `stems_named` — a zip of correctly-named stems that are pure silence (presence-only "includes an X stem") |

Two attacks per document task give the iterator a *gradient*: an item only
`keyword_stuff` passes is a pure rubric-surface match; one `restate_input` also passes
is a brief-echo. Used as subjects via the normal resolver (`attack:<name>` or the bare
name), and surfaced for the iterator via `run_common.attack_subjects` /
`hack_subjects` (nulls ∪ attacks).

```bash
python -m eval.attacks              # (re)generate all; writes eval/attacks/<task>/<attack>/
# score an attack against a rubric like any subject:
python -m eval.run --mode judge --tasks <folder> --subjects gold keyword_stuff restate_input
```

Sanity check on `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` (gpt-mini): gold **0.96**, `keyword_stuff`
**0.89**, `restate_input` **0.74** — ~93% of the gold score is buyable with no
investigation; 44/45 positive items leak to the suite. That is the rubric's hackability
made concrete.

## Running the eval (`--mode judge`)

Judge mode grades one or more **subjects** per task and derives all axes from a
single judging matrix. A subject is named on the CLI:

| subject | meaning |
|---------|---------|
| `gold` | the released gold deliverable (oracle, expect ≈1) |
| `blank`, `unedited_input` | synthesized do-nothing nulls (expect ≈0) |
| `rollout:<policy_key>` | a deliverable produced by `--mode rollout` |
| `preexisting:<path>` | files under `<path>` (or `<path>/<task_folder>`) |

Default subjects are `gold` + the available nulls (the boundary anchors). The axes
fall out of how many models/repeats you ask for:

* **Boundary (Axis 1)** — derived automatically when both `gold` and ≥1 null are
  among the subjects: gold−null **gap** + **leaks** (positive items a null still
  passes — form/presence rewards, reward-hacking suspects).
* **Cross-model consistency (Axis 3)** — turned ON by passing ≥2 `--judge-models`:
  per-subject score spread, pairwise gaps, most-contested items.
* **Self-consistency (Axis 3)** — turned ON by `--repeats > 1`: one judge
  (`--self-model`, default the first judge) re-grades each subject at
  `--temperature`; reports score stdev + fraction of unanimous items.

```bash
# boundary + cross-model in one shot (3 judges, all gold tasks):
python -m eval.run --mode judge --judge-models gpt-mini qwen-vl gemini-lite

# add self-consistency (5 repeats), single task:
python -m eval.run --mode judge --tasks <folder> --judge-models qwen-vl \
    --repeats 5 --temperature 0.7

# consistency OFF (one judge, one pass) on a generated rollout:
python -m eval.run --mode judge --subjects rollout:gpt-mini --judge-models gpt-mini

# grade a directory of preexisting outputs:
python -m eval.run --mode judge --subjects gold preexisting:/path/to/outputs
```
Outputs:
* `results/scorecard.json` — full detail (per-item verdicts, boundary, both
  consistency analyses).
* `results/scorecard.tsv` — one row per (task × subject × judge): `score`,
  `verifiable_only`, `n_unverifiable`, `gold_gap`, `reward_hack_flag`.
* `results/consistency.tsv` — self + cross rows per (task × subject).

## The iterator (`--mode iterate`)

The rubric-improvement loop (take-home Part 3). For each task it runs, per version:

1. **evaluate** — grade `gold` + `hack_subjects` (nulls ∪ attacks) with the judge
   matrix and read off the same axis signals judge mode produces (reusing
   `run_common.leaks` / `.agreement`).
2. **diagnose** (`iterate.diagnose`) — turn those signals into per-item **defects**,
   each tagged with the axis it fails and ranked by *points-at-stake × signal*:
   * `boundary/null_leak` (Axis 1) — a positive item a do-nothing null still passes.
   * `hackability/attack_leak` (Axis 2) — a positive item a mechanical attack
     (`attacks.py`) games: a presence/form/surface-match reward, the classic hack
     surface. The attack that leaks it names the hack kind (brief-echo vs
     rubric-surface vs presence-without-absence).
   * `verifiability/unverifiable` — ≥50% of judges couldn't check the item on gold.
   * `consistency/contested` (Axis 3) — judges disagree on the item (high stdev on
     gold): the wording is ambiguous.
3. **rewrite** (`--rewrite-model`) — a model rewrites **only the flagged criteria**
   to close the named defect (pair presence with absence, make it checkable from the
   artifact, replace vague adjectives with an objective test). Item **ids and point
   values are held fixed**, so every version is graded on the same scale and
   before/after is a clean comparison.
4. **evaluate again** — and stop when defects hit zero or `--rounds` is reached.

It reuses the judge-mode flags for the per-version evaluation config
(`--judge-models` ≥2 → cross-model defects; `--repeats`/`--self-model` → self-consistency;
`--subjects` → which anchors to test; default `gold` + nulls).

```bash
# one rewrite round, cross-model defects from two judges, all gold tasks:
python -m eval.run --mode iterate --judge-models gpt-mini qwen-vl --rounds 1

# a single task, cheaper anchors, two rounds:
python -m eval.run --mode iterate --tasks private_detectives_and_investigators__a46d5cd2 \
    --judge-models gpt-mini gpt-nano --subjects gold blank --rounds 2
```
Outputs:
* `results/rubric_scorecard.tsv` — **one row per (task × rubric version)** with a
  column per axis: `gold_score`, `null_score`, `gold_gap`, `n_leaks` (hackability),
  `cross_model_stdev` / `self_consistency_stdev` (agreement), `n_unverifiable`,
  `verifiability_gap`, `n_defects`, `reward_hack_flag`.
* `rubric_packages/<task>/` — a self-contained package per task: every
  `rubric_v{n}.json`, `diagnosis_v{n}.json` (the defects), `changelog_v{n}_to_v{n+1}.json`
  (what each rewrite changed and why), and `report.json` (before/after at a glance).

> v0 scope: rewrites edit criterion *text* in place (no item add/split yet); Axis 2
> is approximated by the Axis-1 boundary leaks. Both are natural v1 extensions.

## Rollouts (`--mode rollout`)

Have policy model(s) attempt the *real* tasks and produce actual deliverables, so
the axes can be run on genuine model outputs (not just gold/null anchors). The
policy is a tool-using agent in a per-(task, policy) sandbox: it inspects the
inputs with the same modality tools the judge has, then **edits real media** by
driving a sandboxed `ffmpeg` (trim / concat / scale / mux for video & audio), or
writes / renders a document for report tasks. ffmpeg is the only external binary
it may call — no shell, sandbox-relative paths only.

```bash
python -m eval.run --mode rollout --policy-models gpt-mini --tasks <folder>
python -m eval.run --mode rollout --policy-models gpt-mini qwen-vl   # all 10 tasks
```
Writes `eval/rollouts/<task>/<policy>/deliverable/` (+ `rollout.json` trace and
`results/rollouts.tsv`). Grade them by passing `--subjects rollout:<policy>` to
judge mode.

> Note: video/audio rollouts attempt genuine edits, so reliability depends on the
> policy model composing valid ffmpeg steps within the round budget; report tasks
> (PDF) are the most reliable. Either way the output is real, never faked media.

## Cost

Every run prints `[cost] ... ~$X`. A PDF task is ~$0.005–0.01 per judge; a video
task ~$0.02 (image context dominates). Whisper transcripts, frames, and audio
clips are cached under `eval/.cache/` so reruns are cheap. Only 6 of the 10 tasks
have a released gold deliverable, so judge mode defaults to those 6 (rollout mode
defaults to all 10).
```
PDF gold:   private_detectives __57b2cdf2, __a46d5cd2 ; producers_and_directors __e4f664ea
video gold: film_and_video_editors __e222075d, __75401f7c
audio gold: audio_and_video_technicians __38889c3b
```

<type>file</type>
<content>
1: """Unified eval runner — one entrypoint for the rubric scorecard.
2: 
3: Two modes, selected with --mode:
4: 
5:   rollout   Have policy model(s) attempt the real GDPval tasks and produce actual
6:             deliverables (eval.policy). These become gradeable subjects.
7: 
8:   judge     Grade one or more SUBJECTS per task against the rubric, then derive the
9:             axes from a single judging matrix:
10:               * Boundary calibration (Axis 1): gold should score ~1, nulls ~0; we
11:                 report the gold-null gap and "leaks" (null passing a positive item).
12:               * Cross-model consistency (Axis 3): when >=2 --judge-models grade the
13:                 same subject, do they agree?
14:               * Self-consistency (Axis 3): when --repeats > 1, one judge re-grades the
15:                 same subject; do the scores reproduce?
16: 
17: A subject is named on the CLI: `gold`, a null variant (`blank`, `unedited_input`),
18: `rollout:<policy_key>`, or `preexisting:<path>`. Default subjects are gold + nulls
19: (the boundary anchors).
20: 
21: Examples:
22:   # boundary + cross-model in one shot (3 judges, all gold tasks):
23:   python -m eval.run --mode judge --judge-models gpt-mini qwen-vl gemini-lite
24: 
25:   # add self-consistency (one judge, 5 repeats) on a single task:
26:   python -m eval.run --mode judge --tasks private_detectives_and_investigators__57b2cdf2 \
27:       --judge-models qwen-vl --repeats 5 --temperature 0.7
28: 
29:   # consistency OFF (single judge, single pass) on a rollout you generated:
30:   python -m eval.run --mode judge --subjects rollout:gpt-mini --judge-models gpt-mini
31: 
32:   # generate rollouts first:
33:   python -m eval.run --mode rollout --policy-models gpt-mini --tasks <folder>
34: """
35: from __future__ import annotations
36: 
37: import argparse
38: import itertools
39: import json
40: 
41: from . import config, policy, run_common
42: from .config import CostTracker
43: from .harness import judge_safe as judge  # never let one flaky judgement abort the sweep
44: from .models import resolve
45: from .rubric import RubricItem, load_rubric
46: from .run_common import NULL_HACK_SCORE, agreement as _agreement, leaks as _leaks
47: 
48: 
49: # --- judge mode -------------------------------------------------------------
50: def run_judge(tasks, subjects_arg, judge_keys, repeats, self_key, temperature, verbose):
51:     judges = resolve(judge_keys)
52:     self_model = resolve([self_key])[0] if self_key else judges[0]
53:     report: dict = {}
54:     score_rows, cons_rows = [], []
55: 
56:     for tf in tasks:
57:         subjects = subjects_arg or run_common.available_subjects(tf)
58:         if not subjects:
59:             print(f"skip {tf}: no subjects (no gold/nulls; pass --subjects)"); continue
60:         print(f"\n=== {tf} ===  subjects={subjects}")
61:         task = run_common.load_task(tf)
62:         items = load_rubric(tf)
63:         report[tf] = {"subjects": {}}
64: 
65:         # matrix[subject][judge_key] = base run (T=0); matrix[subject]['_self'] = repeats
66:         matrix: dict[str, dict] = {}
67:         for subj in subjects:
68:             try:
69:                 bundle = run_common.resolve_subject(tf, subj)
70:             except SystemExit as e:
71:                 print(f"  skip subject {subj}: {e}"); continue
72:             print(f"  -- subject '{subj}' ({len(bundle.files)} files)")
73:             base = {}
74:             for m in judges:
75:                 r = judge(m, bundle, items, task["prompt"], temperature=0.0, verbose=verbose)
76:                 base[m.key] = r
77:                 vo = r.get("normalized_verifiable_only", r["normalized"])
78:                 flags = ""
79:                 if not r.get("valid", True):
80:                     flags += f" INVALID(scored {r.get('n_items_scored', 0)}/{len(items)})"
81:                 if r.get("penalty_floored"):
82:                     flags += " PENALTY-FLOORED"
83:                 if r.get("error"):
84:                     flags += f" ERROR({r['error'][:40]})"
85:                 print(f"     [{m.key}] score={r['normalized']:.2f} "
86:                       f"(verifiable-only={vo:.2f}, unverifiable={r.get('n_unverifiable', 0)}){flags}")
87:                 score_rows.append([tf, subj, m.key, round(r["normalized"], 3),
88:                                    round(vo, 3), r.get("n_unverifiable", 0), len(items),
89:                                    r.get("valid", True), r.get("penalty_floored", False)])
90:             self_runs = []
91:             if repeats > 1:
92:                 self_runs = [judge(self_model, bundle, items, task["prompt"],
93:                                    temperature=temperature, verbose=verbose)
94:                              for _ in range(repeats)]
95:             matrix[subj] = {"base": base, "self": self_runs}
96:             report[tf]["subjects"][subj] = {
97:                 "is_null": run_common.is_null_subject(tf, subj),
98:                 "base": base,
99:             }
100: 
101:         # ---- derive: self-consistency (per subject) ----
102:         if repeats > 1:
103:             for subj, cell in matrix.items():
104:                 if not cell["self"]:
105:                     continue
106:                 ag = _agreement(items, cell["self"])
107:                 report[tf]["subjects"][subj]["self_consistency"] = {
108:                     "model": self_model.key, "repeats": repeats, "temperature": temperature, **ag}
109:                 print(f"  self-consistency [{self_model.key} x{repeats}] on '{subj}': "
110:                       f"scores={ag['total_scores']} stdev={ag['total_stdev']} "
111:                       f"unanimous={ag['frac_items_unanimous']:.0%}")
112:                 cons_rows.append([tf, subj, "self", f"{self_model.key}x{repeats}",
113:                                   ag["total_mean"], ag["total_stdev"],
114:                                   ag["frac_items_unanimous"], ag["mean_item_stdev"]])
115: 
116:         # ---- derive: cross-model consistency (per subject) ----
117:         if len(judges) > 1:
118:             for subj, cell in matrix.items():
119:                 runs = list(cell["base"].values())
120:                 ag = _agreement(items, runs)
121:                 scores = {k: round(r["normalized"], 3) for k, r in cell["base"].items()}
122:                 pair = {f"{a}-{b}": round(abs(scores[a] - scores[b]), 3)
123:                         for a, b in itertools.combinations(scores, 2)}
124:                 report[tf]["subjects"][subj]["cross_model"] = {
125:                     "models": list(scores), "scores": scores, "pairwise_gap": pair, **ag}
126:                 print(f"  cross-model on '{subj}': scores={scores} "
127:                       f"stdev={ag['total_stdev']} unanimous={ag['frac_items_unanimous']:.0%}")
128:                 cons_rows.append([tf, subj, "cross", "+".join(scores),
129:                                   ag["total_mean"], ag["total_stdev"],
130:                                   ag["frac_items_unanimous"], ag["mean_item_stdev"]])
131: 
132:         # ---- derive: boundary calibration (per judge, needs gold + a null) ----
133:         nulls = [s for s in matrix if report[tf]["subjects"][s]["is_null"]]
134:         if "gold" in matrix and nulls:
135:             report[tf]["boundary"] = {}
136:             for m in judges:
137:                 gold_r = matrix["gold"]["base"][m.key]
138:                 null_runs = [matrix[s]["base"][m.key] for s in nulls]
139:                 worst_null = max((r["normalized"] for r in null_runs), default=0.0)
140:                 gap = gold_r["normalized"] - worst_null
141:                 leaks = _leaks(items, null_runs)
142:                 report[tf]["boundary"][m.key] = {
143:                     "gold": round(gold_r["normalized"], 3),
144:                     "worst_null": round(worst_null, 3), "gap": round(gap, 3),
145:                     "leaks": leaks}
146:                 print(f"  boundary [{m.key}]: gold={gold_r['normalized']:.2f} "
147:                       f"worst_null={worst_null:.2f} gap={gap:+.2f} leaks={len(leaks)}")
148: 
149:     _write_outputs(report, score_rows, cons_rows)
150:     print(CostTracker.summary())
151: 
152: 
153: def _hack_flag(report, tf, subj, judge_key, norm) -> str:
154:     """Mark reward-hacking suspects: leaks on gold, or a null scoring too high."""
155:     is_null = report[tf]["subjects"][subj]["is_null"]
156:     if is_null and norm > NULL_HACK_SCORE:
157:         return "null-scores-high"
158:     if subj == "gold":
159:         n = len(report.get(tf, {}).get("boundary", {}).get(judge_key, {}).get("leaks", []))
160:         if n:
161:             return f"{n}-leaks"
162:     return ""
163: 
164: 
165: def _write_outputs(report, score_rows, cons_rows):
166:     out_json = config.RESULTS / "scorecard.json"
167:     out_json.write_text(json.dumps(report, indent=2))
168:     print(f"\nwrote {out_json.relative_to(config.ROOT)}")
169: 
170:     # annotate score rows with boundary gap + reward-hack flag
171:     full_rows = []
172:     for r in score_rows:
173:         tf, subj, jk, norm = r[0], r[1], r[2], r[3]
174:         gap = ""
175:         if subj == "gold":
176:             b = report.get(tf, {}).get("boundary", {}).get(jk)
177:             if b:
178:                 gap = b["gap"]
179:         full_rows.append(r + [gap, _hack_flag(report, tf, subj, jk, norm)])
180: 
181:     run_common.write_tsv(
182:         config.RESULTS / "scorecard.tsv",
183:         ["task", "subject", "judge", "score", "verifiable_only", "n_unverifiable",
184:          "n_items", "valid", "penalty_floored", "gold_gap", "reward_hack_flag"],
185:         full_rows)
186:     if cons_rows:
187:         run_common.write_tsv(
188:             config.RESULTS / "consistency.tsv",
189:             ["task", "subject", "kind", "judges", "mean_score", "score_stdev",
190:              "frac_unanimous", "mean_item_stdev"],
191:             cons_rows)
192: 
193: 
194: # --- rollout mode -----------------------------------------------------------
195: def run_rollout(tasks, policy_keys, verbose, force=False):
196:     policies = resolve(policy_keys)
197:     rows = []
198:     for tf in tasks:
199:         print(f"\n=== {tf} ===")
200:         for m in policies:
201:             try:
202:                 res = policy.rollout(m, tf, verbose=verbose, force=force)
203:             except Exception as e:  # one model/task failing must not abort the batch
204:                 print(f"  [{m.key}] {tf}: ERROR -> {str(e)[:160]}")
205:                 rows.append([tf, m.key, False, 0, f"(error: {str(e)[:80]})"])
206:                 continue
207:             rows.append([tf, m.key, res["submitted"], res["rounds"],
208:                          "|".join(res["deliverable_files"]) or "(none)"])
209:     run_common.write_tsv(
210:         config.RESULTS / "rollouts.tsv",
211:         ["task", "policy", "submitted", "rounds", "deliverable_files"],
212:         rows)
213:     print(CostTracker.summary())
214:     print("\nGrade them with, e.g.:")
215:     print(f"  python -m eval.run --mode judge --subjects rollout:{policies[0].key} "
216:           f"--judge-models {' '.join(policy_keys)}")
217: 
218: 
219: # --- CLI --------------------------------------------------------------------
220: def main():
221:     ap = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="Unified rubric-scorecard eval runner.")
222:     ap.add_argument("--mode", choices=["judge", "rollout", "iterate"], default="judge")
223:     ap.add_argument("--tasks", nargs="*", default=None,
224:                     help="task folders; default = all (rollout) / all with gold (judge, iterate)")
225:     ap.add_argument("--verbose", action="store_true")
226: 
227:     # judge mode
228:     ap.add_argument("--subjects", nargs="*", default=None,
229:                     help="what to grade: gold | blank | unedited_input | rollout:<key> | "
230:                          "preexisting:<path>. Default = gold + available nulls.")
231:     ap.add_argument("--judge-models", nargs="*", default=["gpt-mini"],
232:                     help=">=2 models turns ON cross-model consistency.")
233:     ap.add_argument("--repeats", type=int, default=1,
234:                     help=">1 turns ON self-consistency (one judge, repeated).")
235:     ap.add_argument("--self-model", default=None,
236:                     help="judge used for self-consistency repeats (default: first --judge-model).")
237:     ap.add_argument("--temperature", type=float, default=0.7,
238:                     help="temperature for self-consistency repeats.")
239: 
240:     # rollout mode
241:     ap.add_argument("--policy-models", nargs="*", default=["gpt-mini"],
242:                     help="model(s) that attempt the tasks to generate deliverables.")
243:     ap.add_argument("--force", action="store_true",
244:                     help="rollout mode: regenerate even if a deliverable already exists.")
245: 
246:     # iterate mode (the rubric-improvement loop; reuses --judge-models / --repeats /
247:     # --self-model / --temperature / --subjects as the per-version evaluation config)
248:     ap.add_argument("--rounds", type=int, default=1,
249:                     help="iterate mode: max rewrite rounds per task (default 1).")
250:     ap.add_argument("--rewrite-model", default="gpt-mini",
251:                     help="iterate mode: model that rewrites flagged criteria.")
252:     a = ap.parse_args()
253: 
254:     if a.mode == "rollout":
255:         tasks = a.tasks or run_common.all_tasks()
256:         run_rollout(tasks, a.policy_models, a.verbose, a.force)
257:     elif a.mode == "iterate":
258:         from . import iterate
259:         tasks = a.tasks or run_common.tasks_with_gold()
260:         iterate.run_iterate(tasks, a.rounds, a.judge_models, a.self_model,
261:                             a.repeats, a.temperature, a.rewrite_model,
262:                             a.subjects, a.verbose)
263:     else:
264:         tasks = a.tasks or run_common.tasks_with_gold()
265:         run_judge(tasks, a.subjects, a.judge_models, a.repeats,
266:                   a.self_model, a.temperature, a.verbose)
267: 
268: 
269: if __name__ == "__main__":
270:     main()

(End of file - total 270 lines)
</content>

                report the gold-null gap and "leaks" (null passing a positive item).
              * Cross-model consistency (Axis 3): when >=2 --judge-models grade the
                same subject, do they agree?
              * Self-consistency (Axis 3): when --repeats > 1, one judge re-grades the
                same subject; do the scores reproduce?

A subject is named on the CLI: `gold`, a null variant (`blank`, `unedited_input`),
`rollout:<policy_key>`, or `preexisting:<path>`. Default subjects are gold + nulls
(the boundary anchors).

display.text:
"""Unified eval runner — one entrypoint for the rubric scorecard.

Two modes, selected with --mode:

  rollout   Have policy model(s) attempt the real GDPval tasks and produce actual
            deliverables (eval.policy). These become gradeable subjects.

  judge     Grade one or more SUBJECTS per task against the rubric, then derive the
            axes from a single judging matrix:
              * Boundary calibration (Axis 1): gold should score ~1, nulls ~0; we
                report the gold-null gap and "leaks" (null passing a positive item).
              * Cross-model consistency (Axis 3): when >=2 --judge-models grade the
                same subject, do they agree?
              * Self-consistency (Axis 3): when --repeats > 1, one judge re-grades the
                same subject; do the scores reproduce?

A subject is named on the CLI: `gold`, a null variant (`blank`, `unedited_input`),
`rollout:<policy_key>`, or `preexisting:<path>`. Default subjects are gold + nulls
(the boundary anchors).

Examples:
  # boundary + cross-model in one shot (3 judges, all gold tasks):
  python -m eval.run --mode judge --judge-models gpt-mini qwen-vl gemini-lite

  # add self-consistency (one judge, 5 repeats) on a single task:
  python -m eval.run --mode judge --tasks private_detectives_and_investigators__57b2cdf2 \
      --judge-models qwen-vl --repeats 5 --temperature 0.7

  # consistency OFF (single judge, single pass) on a rollout you generated:
  python -m eval.run --mode judge --subjects rollout:gpt-mini --judge-models gpt-mini

  # generate rollouts first:
  python -m eval.run --mode rollout --policy-models gpt-mini --tasks <folder>
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import argparse
import itertools
import json

from . import config, policy, run_common
from .config import CostTracker
from .harness import judge_safe as judge  # never let one flaky judgement abort the sweep
from .models import resolve
from .rubric import RubricItem, load_rubric
from .run_common import NULL_HACK_SCORE, agreement as _agreement, leaks as _leaks


# --- judge mode -------------------------------------------------------------
def run_judge(tasks, subjects_arg, judge_keys, repeats, self_key, temperature, verbose):
    judges = resolve(judge_keys)
    self_model = resolve([self_key])[0] if self_key else judges[0]
    report: dict = {}
    score_rows, cons_rows = [], []

    for tf in tasks:
        subjects = subjects_arg or run_common.available_subjects(tf)
        if not subjects:
            print(f"skip {tf}: no subjects (no gold/nulls; pass --subjects)"); continue
        print(f"\n=== {tf} ===  subjects={subjects}")
        task = run_common.load_task(tf)
        items = load_rubric(tf)
        report[tf] = {"subjects": {}}

        # matrix[subject][judge_key] = base run (T=0); matrix[subject]['_self'] = repeats
        matrix: dict[str, dict] = {}
        for subj in subjects:
            try:
                bundle = run_common.resolve_subject(tf, subj)
            except SystemExit as e:
                print(f"  skip subject {subj}: {e}"); continue
            print(f"  -- subject '{subj}' ({len(bundle.files)} files)")
            base = {}
            for m in judges:
                r = judge(m, bundle, items, task["prompt"], temperature=0.0, verbose=verbose)
                base[m.key] = r
                vo = r.get("normalized_verifiable_only", r["normalized"])
                flags = ""
                if not r.get("valid", True):
                    flags += f" INVALID(scored {r.get('n_items_scored', 0)}/{len(items)})"
                if r.get("penalty_floored"):
                    flags += " PENALTY-FLOORED"
                if r.get("error"):
                    flags += f" ERROR({r['error'][:40]})"
                print(f"     [{m.key}] score={r['normalized']:.2f} "
                      f"(verifiable-only={vo:.2f}, unverifiable={r.get('n_unverifiable', 0)}){flags}")
                score_rows.append([tf, subj, m.key, round(r["normalized"], 3),
                                   round(vo, 3), r.get("n_unverifiable", 0), len(items),
                                   r.get("valid", True), r.get("penalty_floored", False)])
            self_runs = []
            if repeats > 1:
                self_runs = [judge(self_model, bundle, items, task["prompt"],
                                   temperature=temperature, verbose=verbose)
                             for _ in range(repeats)]
            matrix[subj] = {"base": base, "self": self_runs}
            report[tf]["subjects"][subj] = {
                "is_null": run_common.is_null_subject(tf, subj),
                "base": base,
            }

        # ---- derive: self-consistency (per subject) ----
        if repeats > 1:
            for subj, cell in matrix.items():
                if not cell["self"]:
                    continue
                ag = _agreement(items, cell["self"])
                report[tf]["subjects"][subj]["self_consistency"] = {
                    "model": self_model.key, "repeats": repeats, "temperature": temperature, **ag}
                print(f"  self-consistency [{self_model.key} x{repeats}] on '{subj}': "
                      f"scores={ag['total_scores']} stdev={ag['total_stdev']} "
                      f"unanimous={ag['frac_items_unanimous']:.0%}")
                cons_rows.append([tf, subj, "self", f"{self_model.key}x{repeats}",
                                  ag["total_mean"], ag["total_stdev"],
                                  ag["frac_items_unanimous"], ag["mean_item_stdev"]])

        # ---- derive: cross-model consistency (per subject) ----
        if len(judges) > 1:
            for subj, cell in matrix.items():
                runs = list(cell["base"].values())
                ag = _agreement(items, runs)
                scores = {k: round(r["normalized"], 3) for k, r in cell["base"].items()}
                pair = {f"{a}-{b}": round(abs(scores[a] - scores[b]), 3)
                        for a, b in itertools.combinations(scores, 2)}
                report[tf]["subjects"][subj]["cross_model"] = {
                    "models": list(scores), "scores": scores, "pairwise_gap": pair, **ag}
                print(f"  cross-model on '{subj}': scores={scores} "
                      f"stdev={ag['total_stdev']} unanimous={ag['frac_items_unanimous']:.0%}")
                cons_rows.append([tf, subj, "cross", "+".join(scores),
                                  ag["total_mean"], ag["total_stdev"],
                                  ag["frac_items_unanimous"], ag["mean_item_stdev"]])

        # ---- derive: boundary calibration (per judge, needs gold + a null) ----
        nulls = [s for s in matrix if report[tf]["subjects"][s]["is_null"]]
        if "gold" in matrix and nulls:
            report[tf]["boundary"] = {}
            for m in judges:
                gold_r = matrix["gold"]["base"][m.key]
                null_runs = [matrix[s]["base"][m.key] for s in nulls]
                worst_null = max((r["normalized"] for r in null_runs), default=0.0)
                gap = gold_r["normalized"] - worst_null
                leaks = _leaks(items, null_runs)
                report[tf]["boundary"][m.key] = {
                    "gold": round(gold_r["normalized"], 3),
                    "worst_null": round(worst_null, 3), "gap": round(gap, 3),
                    "leaks": leaks}
                print(f"  boundary [{m.key}]: gold={gold_r['normalized']:.2f} "
                      f"worst_null={worst_null:.2f} gap={gap:+.2f} leaks={len(leaks)}")

    _write_outputs(report, score_rows, cons_rows)
    print(CostTracker.summary())


def _hack_flag(report, tf, subj, judge_key, norm) -> str:
    """Mark reward-hacking suspects: leaks on gold, or a null scoring too high."""
    is_null = report[tf]["subjects"][subj]["is_null"]
    if is_null and norm > NULL_HACK_SCORE:
        return "null-scores-high"
    if subj == "gold":
        n = len(report.get(tf, {}).get("boundary", {}).get(judge_key, {}).get("leaks", []))
        if n:
            return f"{n}-leaks"
    return ""


def _write_outputs(report, score_rows, cons_rows):
    out_json = config.RESULTS / "scorecard.json"
    out_json.write_text(json.dumps(report, indent=2))
    print(f"\nwrote {out_json.relative_to(config.ROOT)}")

    # annotate score rows with boundary gap + reward-hack flag
    full_rows = []
    for r in score_rows:
        tf, subj, jk, norm = r[0], r[1], r[2], r[3]
        gap = ""
        if subj == "gold":
            b = report.get(tf, {}).get("boundary", {}).get(jk)
            if b:
                gap = b["gap"]
        full_rows.append(r + [gap, _hack_flag(report, tf, subj, jk, norm)])

    run_common.write_tsv(
        config.RESULTS / "scorecard.tsv",
        ["task", "subject", "judge", "score", "verifiable_only", "n_unverifiable",
         "n_items", "valid", "penalty_floored", "gold_gap", "reward_hack_flag"],
        full_rows)
    if cons_rows:
        run_common.write_tsv(
            config.RESULTS / "consistency.tsv",
            ["task", "subject", "kind", "judges", "mean_score", "score_stdev",
             "frac_unanimous", "mean_item_stdev"],
            cons_rows)


# --- rollout mode -----------------------------------------------------------
def run_rollout(tasks, policy_keys, verbose, force=False):
    policies = resolve(policy_keys)
    rows = []
    for tf in tasks:
        print(f"\n=== {tf} ===")
        for m in policies:
            try:
                res = policy.rollout(m, tf, verbose=verbose, force=force)
            except Exception as e:  # one model/task failing must not abort the batch
                print(f"  [{m.key}] {tf}: ERROR -> {str(e)[:160]}")
                rows.append([tf, m.key, False, 0, f"(error: {str(e)[:80]})"])
                continue
            rows.append([tf, m.key, res["submitted"], res["rounds"],
                         "|".join(res["deliverable_files"]) or "(none)"])
    run_common.write_tsv(
        config.RESULTS / "rollouts.tsv",
        ["task", "policy", "submitted", "rounds", "deliverable_files"],
        rows)
    print(CostTracker.summary())
    print("\nGrade them with, e.g.:")
    print(f"  python -m eval.run --mode judge --subjects rollout:{policies[0].key} "
          f"--judge-models {' '.join(policy_keys)}")


# --- CLI --------------------------------------------------------------------
def main():
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="Unified rubric-scorecard eval runner.")
    ap.add_argument("--mode", choices=["judge", "rollout", "iterate"], default="judge")
    ap.add_argument("--tasks", nargs="*", default=None,
                    help="task folders; default = all (rollout) / all with gold (judge, iterate)")
    ap.add_argument("--verbose", action="store_true")

    # judge mode
    ap.add_argument("--subjects", nargs="*", default=None,
                    help="what to grade: gold | blank | unedited_input | rollout:<key> | "
                         "preexisting:<path>. Default = gold + available nulls.")
    ap.add_argument("--judge-models", nargs="*", default=["gpt-mini"],
                    help=">=2 models turns ON cross-model consistency.")
    ap.add_argument("--repeats", type=int, default=1,
                    help=">1 turns ON self-consistency (one judge, repeated).")
    ap.add_argument("--self-model", default=None,
                    help="judge used for self-consistency repeats (default: first --judge-model).")
    ap.add_argument("--temperature", type=float, default=0.7,
                    help="temperature for self-consistency repeats.")

    # rollout mode
    ap.add_argument("--policy-models", nargs="*", default=["gpt-mini"],
                    help="model(s) that attempt the tasks to generate deliverables.")
    ap.add_argument("--force", action="store_true",
                    help="rollout mode: regenerate even if a deliverable already exists.")

    # iterate mode (the rubric-improvement loop; reuses --judge-models / --repeats /
    # --self-model / --temperature / --subjects as the per-version evaluation config)
    ap.add_argument("--rounds", type=int, default=1,
                    help="iterate mode: max rewrite rounds per task (default 1).")
    ap.add_argument("--rewrite-model", default="gpt-mini",
                    help="iterate mode: model that rewrites flagged criteria.")
    a = ap.parse_args()

    if a.mode == "rollout":
        tasks = a.tasks or run_common.all_tasks()
        run_rollout(tasks, a.policy_models, a.verbose, a.force)
    elif a.mode == "iterate":
        from . import iterate
        tasks = a.tasks or run_common.tasks_with_gold()
        iterate.run_iterate(tasks, a.rounds, a.judge_models, a.self_model,
                            a.repeats, a.temperature, a.rewrite_model,
                            a.subjects, a.verbose)
    else:
        tasks = a.tasks or run_common.tasks_with_gold()
        run_judge(tasks, a.subjects, a.judge_models, a.repeats,
                  a.self_model, a.temperature, a.verbose)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

bundle: Bundle,
    items: list[RubricItem],
    task_prompt: str,
    temperature: float = 0.0,
    verbose: bool = False,
    protocol: str = "",
) -> dict:
    """Run the agent loop. Returns normalize(...) dict plus raw notes/meta.

    `protocol` is a per-rubric grading protocol: task-specific verification rules
    that travel WITH the rubric version (the iterator can evolve them). They are
    part of the reward function — e.g. "for any stated fact, confirm it against the
    reference log; do not credit a bare assertion" — and are how the loop closes
    hacks that no criterion-text edit can reach (a transcribe-the-rubric attack)."""
    del_list = "\n".join(f"- {f['name']} [{f['type']}, {f['size_kb']}KB]"
                         for f in bundle.listing(role="deliverable")) or "(empty — a do-nothing output)"

display.text:
bundle: Bundle,
    items: list[RubricItem],
    task_prompt: str,
    temperature: float = 0.0,
    verbose: bool = False,
    protocol: str = "",
) -> dict:
    """Run the agent loop. Returns normalize(...) dict plus raw notes/meta.

    `protocol` is a per-rubric grading protocol: task-specific verification rules
    that travel WITH the rubric version (the iterator can evolve them). They are
    part of the reward function — e.g. "for any stated fact, confirm it against the
    reference log; do not credit a bare assertion" — and are how the loop closes
    hacks that no criterion-text edit can reach (a transcribe-the-rubric attack)."""
    del_list = "\n".join(f"- {f['name']} [{f['type']}, {f['size_kb']}KB]"
                         for f in bundle.listing(role="deliverable")) or "(empty — a do-nothing output)"
    ref_files = bundle.listing(role="reference")
    ref_block = ""
    if ref_files:
        ref_list = "\n".join(f"- {f['name']} [{f['type']}, {f['size_kb']}KB]" for f in ref_files)
        ref_block = (
            "\n\nREFERENCE INPUTS (what the worker was GIVEN — NOT the deliverable, never "
            "gradeable; consult only for comparison criteria via read_reference/view_reference/"
            "compare_audio_sync):\n" + ref_list)
    user = (
        f"TASK GIVEN TO THE WORKER:\n{task_prompt.strip()}\n\n"
        f"DELIVERABLE TO GRADE (bundle '{bundle.label}') — grade ONLY these files. Award credit "
        f"only for what is present IN the deliverable itself, never for content that appears only "
        f"in a reference input:\n{del_list}{ref_block}\n\n"
        f"Inspect with the tools, then grade every rubric item below.\n\n"

It is a tool-using agent working inside a per-(task, policy) sandbox:

parts.text: Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me compile all the findings into a clear report.

Here is a thorough report of all findings.

---

## 1. Aggregated Scorecard: `eval/results/rubric_scorecard.tsv`

**File:** `/Users/albert/Projects/philo/eval/results/rubric_scorecard.tsv`

This TSV contains 3 lines total (header + 2 data rows), one row per (task x rubric version):

| Row | task | version | gold_score | null_score | gold_gap | n_leaks | n_attack_leaks | worst_attack_score | margin | objective | self_consistency_stdev | cross_model_stdev | n_unverifiable | verifiability_gap | n_defects | reward_hack_flag |
|-----|------|---------|------------|------------|----------|---------|----------------|-------------------|--------|-----------|------------------------|-------------------|----------------|-------------------|-----------|-----------------|
| 1 | `private_detectives_and_investigators__57b2cdf2` | v0 | 0.915 | (empty) | (empty) | 0 | 44 | 0.864 | 0.051 | 0.047 | 0.008 | (empty) | 0 | 0.0 | 44 | `attack-leaks` |
| 2 | same task | v1 | 0.932 | (empty) | (empty) | 0 | 44 | 0.89 | 0.042 | 0.03 | 0.025 | (empty) | 0 | 0.0 | 44 | `attack-leaks` |

**Key observations:**
- Only one task has been iterated (`57b2cdf2`), with 2 versions (v0 and v1).
- The iterator ran only 1 round (v0 -> v1, then stopped).
- Both versions still flag `attack-leaks`, meaning the mechanical attacks still game positive items.
- The **objective** (the loop's hill-climbing metric = `margin - 0.5 * self_consistency_stdev`) **dropped** from 0.047 (v0) to 0.03 (v1), which is why the loop stopped -- v1 was **rejected** and the loop reverted to v0 (the best version).
- `n_attack_leaks` stayed constant at 44 across both versions -- the rewrites did not reduce the number of leaking items.

---

## 2. Rubric Packages Under `eval/rubric_packages/`

**Directory:** `/Users/albert/Projects/philo/eval/rubric_packages/`

**2 entries** (subdirectories):

| Package Directory | Contents |
|---|---|
| `private_detectives_and_investigators__57b2cdf2/` | `rubric_v0.json`, `rubric_v1.json`, `rubric_best.json`, `diagnosis_v0.json`, `diagnosis_v1.json`, `changelog_v0_to_v1.json`, `report.json`, `protocol_v0.txt`, `protocol_v1.txt`, `protocol_best.txt` |
| `private_detectives_and_investigators__a46d5cd2/` | `rubric_v0.json`, `rubric_v1.json`, `diagnosis_v0.json`, `diagnosis_v1.json`, `changelog_v0_to_v1.json`, `report.json` (no protocol files -- no attacks were used, just nulls) |

Each package contains:
- **`rubric_v{n}.json`** -- the rubric at each version
- **`diagnosis_v{n}.json`** -- headline metrics + per-item defects for that version
- **`changelog_v{n}_to_v{n+1}.json`** -- what the rewriter changed and why
- **`report.json`** -- before/after summary with all versions' headlines and top defects
- **`rubric_best.json`** / **`protocol_best.txt`** -- the chosen best version (the one the iterator would use as the reward function)

---

## 3. Definitions and Descriptions of "Reward Hacking"

### 3a. `eval/attacks.py` -- The mechanical attack suite (Axis 2)

**File:** `/Users/albert/Projects/philo/eval/attacks.py`

- **Line 1**: Docstring header: `"""Mechanically-generated reward-hacking deliverables for Axis 2 (hackability).`
- **Lines 3-6**: Explains the purpose: "Where `null_inputs` builds *do-nothing* outputs ... this builds *cheap-but-targeted* attacks: deliverables that try to raise the rubric score WITHOUT doing the real work. They are the empirical core of the hackability axis -- the "attack suite" the iterator scores a rubric against."
- **Lines 8-10**: "An attack passing a positive item is a `leak`: the item rewards surface form, not quality, and is a rewrite target."
- **Lines 333-353**: Describes the v0 adversarial agent (LLM-based), whose "objective is reward hacking itself -- compose a deliverable that scores HIGH on the rubric yet is substantively WORSE than the gold."

**Attack families defined** (lines 13-39):
| Attack | Type | Mechanism |
|--------|------|-----------|
| `restate_input` | document (PDF) | Echo the brief/prompt + reference files verbatim |
| `keyword_stuff` | document (PDF) | Emit every quoted literal + de-verbed criterion (rubric as its own answer key) |
| `presence_max` | video (mp4) | Oversized title + caption bar covering the frame |
| `stems_named` | archive (zip) | Correctly-named but silent audio stems |

### 3b. `eval/iterate.py` -- The reward_hack_flag and hack kind descriptions

**File:** `/Users/albert/Projects/philo/eval/iterate.py`

- **Lines 12-14**: v0 scope: "Axes wired in: ... Axis 2 (reward-hackability, via the mechanical attack suite in attacks.py -- a positive item a cheap attack games is a rewrite target, and the leaking attack names the hack pattern)"
- **Lines 39-48**: `_HACK_KIND` dictionary mapping attack names to human-readable hack descriptions:
  - `restate_input`: "the fact is already in the brief, so restating an input earns the point (brief-echo, not work done)"
  - `keyword_stuff`: "a document that merely transcribes the rubric's own wording earns the point (rubric-surface match / self-certification)"
  - `presence_max`: "an oversized title/caption covering the subject earns the point (presence with no matching absence condition)"
  - `stems_named`: "a correctly-named but silent/empty stem earns the point (presence-only, no quality check)"
- **Lines 120-132**: Axis 2 evaluation: mechanical attack subject grading and leak detection.
- **Lines 447-449**: `reward_hack_flag` computation:
  ```python
  "reward_hack_flag": "attack-leaks" if hk.get("leaks") else
                      ("null-leaks" if b.get("leaks") else
                       ("null-high" if (b.get("worst_null") or 0) > run_common.NULL_HACK_SCORE else "")),
  ```
  Three severity levels: `attack-leaks` (worst), `null-leaks`, `null-high`.

### 3c. `eval/run_common.py` -- LEAK_THRESHOLD and NULL_HACK_SCORE

**File:** `/Users/albert/Projects/philo/eval/run_common.py`

- **Lines 16-17**: Thresholds:
  ```python
  LEAK_THRESHOLD = 0.5      # a null earning >=50% of a positive item's points = leak
  NULL_HACK_SCORE = 0.30    # a null scoring above this is a reward-hacking suspect
  ```
- **Lines 188-198**: `leaks()` function: identifies positive items where a null/attack earns >=50% of the points.

### 3d. `eval/README.md` -- Documentation

- **Lines 102-133**: Section "Attack suite (`attacks.py`) -- Axis 2 (hackability)" explains the philosophy.
- **Line 130-133**: Sanity check: `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` with gpt-mini yields gold 0.96, keyword_stuff 0.89, restate_input 0.74 -- "~93% of the gold score is buyable with no investigation; 44/45 positive items leak to the suite. That is the rubric's hackability made concrete."
- **Lines 180-226**: Documents the iterator loop, including how `hackability/attack_leak` defects are diagnosed.

### 3e. `eval/run.py` -- reward_hack_flag in judge mode

- **Lines 153-162**: `_hack_flag()` function annotates each scorecard row with `reward_hack_flag` based on null scores and boundary leaks.

### 3f. Instructions file

**File:** `/Users/albert/Projects/philo/instructions.md`
- **Line 139**: "flagging suspected reward hacking"
- **Line 150**: "makes a rubric trustworthy as training signal, and clear thinking about reward hacking"

---

## 4. What "Iterator" Refers To

The **iterator** is defined in `eval/iterate.py` and is the **rubric improvement loop** (Part 3 of the eval system). Here are all references:

### 4a. `eval/iterate.py` (primary definition)

**File:** `/Users/albert/Projects/philo/eval/iterate.py`

- **Line 1**: `"""The rubric iterator (Part 3): a loop that improves a rubric automatically.`
- **Lines 3-9**: The loop structure:
  ```
  evaluate -> grade gold + nulls with the judge matrix and read off axis signals
  diagnose -> turn signals into per-item DEFECTS
  rewrite  -> ask a model to rewrite ONLY flagged criteria to close gaps
  evaluate -> grade the rewritten rubric and report what moved
  ```
- **Line 18**: "A richer iterator could split or add items -- left for v1."
- **Lines 453-542**: `iterate_task()` function: the core loop that runs evaluate/diagnose/rewrite for one task, hill-climbs on the objective, reverts backfires, and writes the package.

## 5. Before/After Comparison: Scorecard History and Baseline Data

### 5a. The primary before/after artifact: `report.json`

Each rubric package contains a `report.json` with the before/after summary:

**`/Users/albert/Projects/philo/eval/rubric_packages/private_detectives_and_investigators__57b2cdf2/report.json`** (lines 14-139):
- v0: gold=0.915, worst_attack=0.864, margin=0.051, objective=0.047, 44 defects, `attack-leaks`
- v1: gold=0.932, worst_attack=0.89, margin=0.042, objective=0.03, 44 defects, `attack-leaks`
- **Result:** v1 was **rejected** (objective dropped from 0.047 to 0.03), so the loop reverted to v0. A `rubric_best.json` and `protocol_best.txt` were written with the v0 values.

**`/Users/albert/Projects/philo/eval/rubric_packages/private_detectives_and_investigators__a46d5cd2/report.json`** (lines 12-129):
- v0: gold=0.678, null_score=0.027, gold_gap=0.651, 1 null leak, 17 defects, `leaks`
- v1: gold=0.664, null_score=0.0, gold_gap=0.664, 0 null leaks, 9 defects, `(empty)` -- no reward_hack_flag
- **Result:** v1 was **accepted** (no objective was computed since there were no attack subjects; the loop ran its 1 round then stopped when `n_defects < MAX_REWRITES_PER_ROUND`). The null leak was fixed, consistency defects reduced.

### 5b. Diagnosis files show the per-item defect evolution

Each `diagnosis_v{n}.json` records the full per-item defect list:

**`57b2cdf2`**:
- v0 (`diagnosis_v0.json`): 44 `hackability`/`attack_leak` defects -- items like "Provides the deliverable as a PDF" that both attacks pass at 100%.
- v1 (`diagnosis_v1.json`): Still 44 defects, but criteria were edited to add quality conditions (e.g., "Provides the deliverable as a PDF, and the PDF is a complete report rather than a placeholder"). However, attacks still leaked -- most items still at 100% null_fraction.

**`a46d5cd2`**:
- v0 (`diagnosis_v0.json`): 17 defects -- 1 `boundary`/`null_leak` + 16 `consistency`/`contested`. The null leak was "Delivers the final report as a single PDF document."
- v1 (`diagnosis_v1.json`): 9 defects -- all `consistency`/`contested` (the null leak was fixed, consistency improved but not eliminated).

### 5c. Changelogs show exactly what changed

**`57b2cdf2/changelog_v0_to_v1.json`**: 8 criterion rewrites + 1 protocol change. The rewriter added quality/absence conditions to section labels, order checks, and header items. A grading protocol was introduced (first time) instructing judges to verify claims against the report itself and not award points for bare assertions.

**`a46d5cd2/changelog_v0_to_v1.json`**: 8 criterion edits. Fixed the null-leak PDF criterion ("adds a quality/anti-null condition") and 7 consistency defects (e.g., replaced "six months prior" with "approximately six months ... acceptable range: 5 to 7 months").

### 5d. Scorecard TSV as the master rollup

The `rubric_scorecard.tsv` provides the aggregate view: one row per (task x version) with all axis metrics. Currently only 2 rows (one task, 2 versions). No baseline version is kept separately -- v0 serves as the implicit baseline for each task.

### 5e. No separate "baseline" or "history" directory

There is no independent baseline archive or historical database. The before/after story is fully contained in each `rubric_packages/<task>/` directory:
- `rubric_v0.json` = baseline rubric
- `diagnosis_v0.json` = baseline metrics + defects
- `rubric_v1.json` and `diagnosis_v1.json` = after one rewrite round
- `report.json` = side-by-side summary
- `changelog_v0_to_v1.json` = exact diffs with rationale

## Summary: Are Reward Hacking Suites Positively Helping the Iterator?

**Yes, but with mixed results:**

1. **The attack suite successfully identifies reward-hacking surfaces.** The mechanical attacks (`restate_input`, `keyword_stuff`) empirically demonstrate that 44/45 rubric items leak -- i.e., the rubric can be gamed without real work. The `reward_hack_flag` of `attack-leaks` on every version confirms the suite's diagnostic value.

2. **The iterator uses leak data to drive rewrites.** The `diagnose()` function (lines 204-225 of `iterate.py`) converts attack leaks into `hackability`/`attack_leak` defects with specific guidance citing which attack exposes which pattern (brief-echo vs rubric-surface-match vs presence-without-absence). The rewrites target exactly these.

3. **The rewrites improve criteria text** to pair presence conditions with absence/quality requirements (documented in the changelogs). For `57b2cdf2`, the rewriter also generated a **grading protocol** for the first time -- a cross-cutting defense against keyword-stuffing attacks that no criterion edit alone can fix.

4. **However, the attack suite is a *stress test*, not a panacea.** In the `57b2cdf2` case, v1 actually *backfired* -- the objective dropped from 0.047 to 0.03 because gold rose more slowly than the worst attack score (gold: 0.915->0.932, worst_attack: 0.864->0.890), narrowing the margin. The hill-climbing loop correctly rejected v1 and reverted to v0, demonstrating the safety mechanism works.

5. **The `a46d5cd2` task (which only used nulls, not attacks) showed unambiguous improvement**: null leaks eliminated (0->0), cross-model stdev reduced (0.13->0.062), defects cut from 17 to 9. This case had less severe reward-hacking problems to begin with.

**Bottom line:** The reward hacking suites are **positively helping** by giving the iterator concrete, item-specific signals about which criteria are gameable, with named hack patterns that guide rewriting. The loop's hill-climbing objective prevents backfires from being accepted. The attack suite is the reason the iterator can target reward hacking specifically rather than making blind edits.
